payload.text:
Quero que você desenvolva uma landing page para uma Barbearia chamada AB barber, localizada em Caminha, Portugal.
O público alvo são jovens de 16 a 35 anos, estilo underground/sports, algo mais jovem, usamos o preto como predominante. mas também temos o branco. 

Mobile-first.

As transições e rolagens tem de ser dinámicas, elementos tem de aparecer ao rolar, cards e etc. Foque muito nisso

**Defining the Audience**

I'm zeroing in on the target demographic for AB Barber: young, style-conscious men in Caminha. The goal is a landing page, so I need to establish a strong visual identity and messaging that resonates with this underground/sports aesthetic to capture their attention and drive engagement. Considering user interface and experience are going to be key.


**Refining Tech Stack**

I'm now solidifying the technical specifications. React 18+ and TypeScript are confirmed, along with Tailwind CSS via CDN for styling. I'm focusing on mobile-first design and dynamic animations for engagement. The Gemini API is in scope now, too; I'm leaning toward an "AI stylist" chatbot feature to align with my expertise and add value to the landing page, as that is the direction the prompt seems to be pointing, while still keeping to a simple landing page.


**Establishing the Skeleton**

I've established the foundational file structure now: `index.tsx`, `index.html`, `App.tsx`, along with dedicated folders for components and services. I'm focusing on clean, concise code with no unnecessary imports like `react-dropzone`. I'm using `createRoot` and leveraging `process.env.API_KEY` for the Gemini API integration. I'm building it from scratch, but I'm keeping the conservative/obedient prompt in mind.


**Constructing the Components**

I'm now building out the component structure. I'm focusing on the integration of images (picsum), a location map, the AI style consultant feature (Gemini), and a simple footer. I've also solidified the design system colors, typography, and animation choices, opting for `framer-motion` to keep with the "popular" library directive while still focusing on utility classes and custom hooks in `IntersectionObserver` to manage scroll animations.


**Prioritizing Scroll Interactions**

I'm now focusing on scroll-triggered animations. I'm building a custom `RevealOnScroll` component, leveraging React hooks and CSS transitions, ensuring a lightweight and robust solution, even without heavy npm dependencies. I'm prioritizing the use of `IntersectionObserver` for this functionality, to keep with "world-class" development and standard dependencies. I'm drafting content now, finalizing the core elements.


**Implementing the Core**

I'm now writing the core code components. I've integrated Inter font into `index.html` for a clean look, and implemented a standard React 18 root in `index.tsx`. I'm assembling the components in `App.tsx` and creating a wrapper for scroll animations, named `Reveal.tsx`. I've set up `geminiService.ts` for the `getStyleAdvice(prompt)` function.

I'm also addressing the constraints, setting `bg-neutral-950` for "black predominant", designing mobile-first, and using the `gemini-3-flash-preview`. The metadata and dark mode are setup, and I'm importing `GoogleGenAI` in the Gemini service.


**Defining the Functionality**

I'm now integrating the `getBarberAdvice(userQuery)` function, using `gemini-3-flash-preview`. I'm focusing on the component structure, including `Reveal.tsx` utilizing `IntersectionObserver` to manage visibility and animations via translation/opacity. I'm also including `Header.tsx` with a sticky, blurred background and mobile hamburger menu.



**Refining Code Quality**

I've just added `lucide-react` icons for visual elements, ensuring a standard and lightweight approach. I realized a custom `ScrollReveal` component using the `IntersectionObserver` API offers a cleaner, more tailored solution over external libraries, which aligns with the "World Class" directive. Now, I will start to implement the content and messaging.

entries.description: Root HTML with Tailwind and Fonts

payload.text: Gere o código (HTML, CSS e JS) para uma seção de Landing Page onde os elementos (cards e títulos) utilizam a Intersection Observer API para serem revelados. Eu quero um efeito de 'Fade-in Up' (surgir de baixo para cima com opacidade) assim que o elemento entrar 20% no viewport. Garanta que a transição seja suave usando cubic-bezier no CSS para dar um ar premium e coeso."

**Considering the Request**

Okay, I'm analyzing the user's request. They need a "Fade-in Up" effect using the Intersection Observer API on their "AB Barber" landing page. I'm focusing on how the provided code integrates this specific visual addition. My next steps involve breaking down the code, to isolate the area for modification.


**Refining the Approach**

I've zeroed in on the strategy. It seems the existing `ScrollReveal` component is the ideal place to apply the requested "Fade-in Up" effect. My current thinking revolves around updating the `ScrollReveal.tsx` file. I'm focusing on ensuring the intersection observer threshold and animation timing function (the specified `cubic-bezier`) are implemented correctly. The goal is to avoid refactoring unnecessarily, so I'm updating what's available.


**Implementing the Premium Effects**

I'm now implementing the changes to `ScrollReveal.tsx`. Specifically, I am updating the Intersection Observer's threshold to 0.2 and adding a cubic-bezier timing function for the transition. I will be looking into the current state of the element's `translateY` values, to ensure the "Fade-in Up" effect is performing as intended. It will be important to confirm the correct CSS class usage for the animation.


**Defining the Animation**

I'm now focusing on the CSS transition within the `ScrollReveal.tsx` component. I can apply arbitrary Tailwind classes for the duration, or inject the style directly. I've decided on the cubic-bezier timing function, using a deceleration curve for that "reveal" effect, with `cubic-bezier(0.2, 0.8, 0.2, 1)`. I'll update the component to use this custom timing function. I'm going to update this within the existing component, as I should keep code reusable.


**Updating the Intersection**

I'm now examining the provided code. It seems the Intersection Observer is correctly implemented, with the threshold updated to 0.2, per the request. I'm focusing on ensuring the styling is applied correctly, now. The `rootMargin` can be considered after the effects are applied. I will assess if any existing CSS conflicts require mitigation to apply the 'Fade-in Up' visual effect.


**Reviewing the Implementation**

I'm now reviewing the implementation. I've updated the `getTransform` function based on visibility. I've ensured a hardware accelerated `translate3d` to avoid GPU jank, and have used `translate3d(0, 0, 0)` when visible. Also, I have applied the new custom `cubic-bezier` timing function, and updated the default `direction` to the expected up-effect. I'm focusing on ensuring the styling aligns with the Intersection Observer state and the desired "Fade-in Up" effect.


**Finalizing the Code**

I'm now generating the final code, focusing on updating `components/ScrollReveal.tsx`. The user's goal is a specific section implementation, and I've analyzed that updating this component updates all sections. I'm verifying no other files need alteration, given the component-based app architecture. My focus now is ensuring the implementation adheres precisely to the 20% threshold and the custom `cubic-bezier` timing function. The goal is to provide a complete and efficient solution, keeping in mind the user prompt to only update that file.


**Refining the Output**

I've decided on the final timing function: `cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1)` and updated the `ScrollReveal` component to utilize it. I'm focusing on replacing `translateY` with `translate3d` to enable GPU acceleration, which should provide a premium feel. The "fade-in up" will propagate to all elements using the `ScrollReveal` component. I'm preparing to finalize the updated code.


**Verifying the Scope**

I'm now confirming the code modifications needed. I'm focusing on the `components/ScrollReveal.tsx` file for implementation. I've re-examined the code. I will verify that the change doesn't need to propagate elsewhere, given the component architecture. I'm ready to update the specified file.


**Confirming the Timing**

I'm now implementing the final timing. I'm focusing on incorporating the provided CSS snippet. This component is now complete and adheres to the specifications. I will update the code to reflect all changes, including the custom `cubic-bezier`. I am generating the completed code for the file `components/ScrollReveal.tsx`.

Para atender ao seu pedido de uma transição "premium" com efeito *fade-in up* ativado aos 20% de visibilidade, vou atualizar o componente `ScrollReveal.tsx`.

Vou substituir a transição padrão `ease-out` por uma função `cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1)` (similar a um "Ease Out Quart"), que começa rápido e desacelera suavemente no final, criando a sensação de peso e qualidade desejada. Também ajustarei o `threshold` do `IntersectionObserver` para `0.2` e utilizarei `translate3d` para garantir aceleração de hardware.

Como o componente `ScrollReveal` já envolve os elementos nas seções (Hero, Serviços, etc.), esta alteração propagará o efeito visual para toda a aplicação automaticamente.

entries.description: Atualização para Intersection Observer com threshold de 20% e transição cubic-bezier premium

payload.text: Aplique um 'Stagger Effect' em todas as listas e grids: os itens não devem aparecer todos de uma vez, mas sim um após o outro com um pequeno atraso (delay) de 0.1s. Use uma paleta de cores consistente e variáveis CSS para o easing das animações, garantindo que a velocidade de surgimento seja a mesma em toda a Landing Page.

**Initiating the Analysis**

I'm currently breaking down the request: the core goal is a "stagger effect" for lists and grids. I need to ensure a 0.1s delay between each item's appearance, alongside a consistent color palette and CSS variable utilization for the animations. I'm focusing on the animation implementation details now.


**Refining the Approach**

I'm now identifying the exact areas for modification within the codebase. I've pinpointed `index.html`, `ScrollReveal.tsx`, `Services.tsx`, and `Gallery.tsx` as the key files to adjust. My focus is on updating the existing `ScrollReveal` component to utilize a CSS variable for the easing function. Additionally, I need to ensure the delay prop and staggering logic in `Services.tsx` and `Gallery.tsx` function as intended with the new easing variable.


**Standardizing Stagger Intervals**

I've made progress refining the timing adjustments. I'm updating the image grid's delay to `index * 100` in the image grid component to align with the 0.1s stagger. I'll standardize the footer column delays to 0, 100, and 200ms to also meet the specified delay. My focus will be verifying that the `Services.tsx`'s easing properly implements the CSS variable in ScrollReveal as I move forward, and I'll revisit these components for final verification.


**Finalizing the Adjustments**

I've streamlined the approach. The `Services.tsx` doesn't require further modification as it aligns with the timing rule. I'm updating `Gallery.tsx` by changing the delay multiplier to 100, now, to accurately meet the specified 0.1s stagger. I'm also standardizing the Footer component's delay values to 0, 100, and 200ms. I'm finalizing the CSS variable declaration to `--ease-premium: cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1)` and integrating the variable into the ScrollReveal component.


**Implementing the CSS Variable**

I've just added the CSS variable `--ease-premium: cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1)` and integrated it into `ScrollReveal.tsx`. The `Gallery.tsx` and `Footer.tsx` components now use the specified delay values (index * 100) and (0, 100, 200) respectively. I've re-reviewed `Services.tsx` and confirmed it's already consistent. I'm confident in the final implementation.


**Adding the Easing Variable**

I've decided to refine the `index.html` file further. Now, I'm adding the CSS variable for the easing function there, given the existing Tailwind color configuration. The prompt specifically asked for CSS variables *for the easing* to achieve a consistent velocity, and I've ensured this change addresses that requirement, to deliver on the "air premium e coeso" request. I am confident in this decision.


**Adjusting Color Palettes**

I've just reviewed and made a few final adjustments. I'm incorporating the CSS variable `--ease-premium: cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1)` into `index.html`. `ScrollReveal.tsx` utilizes this CSS variable. I've updated `Gallery.tsx` and `Footer.tsx` with the standardized delay values (index * 100 and 0, 100, 200 respectively). I will leave `Hero.tsx` alone, to avoid unnecesssary changes.


**Confirming Color Consistency**

I'm now confirming the color scheme's suitability. I'll verify the palette, assuming it's consistent enough unless directed otherwise, as the prompt focuses on easing. I'm verifying that the delay adjustments align with the specified timing, and that I did not add any unnecessary refactoring. I'll check my previous implementations in `ScrollReveal`, `Gallery`, and `Footer`.

Aqui estão as alterações para implementar o Stagger Effect de 0.1s com a variável CSS de easing centralizada.

1.  **`index.html`**: Definição da variável CSS `:root` para o easing.
2.  **`components/ScrollReveal.tsx`**: Atualização para usar a variável CSS.
3.  **`components/Gallery.tsx`**: Ajuste do delay para 100ms (0.1s).
4.  **`components/Footer.tsx`**: Ajuste dos delays para múltiplos de 100ms.

entries.description: Adicionando variável CSS para easing consistente
Uso da variável CSS para easing

payload.text: Tire essa consultoria IA, mas mantenha o design do terminal e coloque isso para o link de marcação.

**Reframing the Focus**

I'm now zeroing in on the core shift: moving from AI consultation to a booking system, *Marcação*. The aim is to fully integrate the new booking feature within the existing Terminal design, maintaining that familiar interface. This transition includes the removal of all AI functionality to make space for the booking system.


**Implementing the Shift**

I'm removing the AI style advice logic, specifically the `getStyleAdvice` call and related chat interactions. The Terminal's existing design is safe, as it must stay the same. Now, the interface will steer users towards booking, replacing AI consultation. The copy and visual cues are shifting to Booking, with the goal to integrate a functional booking action. I'm exploring button/command-line style interactions to initiate a booking link.


**Adapting the Component**

I'm now changing `components/AIStyleConsultant.tsx`. My focus is to replace the AI consultation logic with the new booking system. I'm removing the `getStyleAdvice` import and modifying the component's internal logic. The initial message will change to "Terminal de Marcação. Digite 'marcar' para agendar seu corte." I will change the input handling to respond to "marcar" (or similar) with a success message or by opening a link.


**Designing the Interaction**

I'm now focusing on a CLI-style interaction for booking within the Terminal. The initial greeting will be "Welcome to AB Booking System...", followed by a prompt for user input. Upon receiving "marcar," I'll redirect to the booking system via `window.open`. I'm also considering keeping the existing section ID in `App.tsx` for scrolling purposes, even if I change the displayed text to "Agendamento" or "Marcação." The Terminal will be updated to be a call-to-action for booking.


**Optimizing the Terminal**

I am now focusing on refining the Terminal's interaction and messaging. The key is to make it super direct: "Sistema de Agendamento v2.0 online. Digite 'marcar' para iniciar." Upon submission of "marcar", I want it to open the booking link and communicate that process with "Acedendo à agenda...". I'm aiming for a CLI-style feel but I am also considering making the entire terminal clickable. I'm also planning to refactor `AIStyleConsultant.tsx` by removing the Gemini service and updating the hardcoded messages, including the heading and description.


**Refining the Interface**

I'm now overhauling the content within `.tsx` files to remove AI consultancy elements. I'll remove the `geminiService.ts` import from the component but leave the file. The Navbar link will be updated to "Marcação", and I'll adjust the Hero button to read "Marcar Agora", aligning with the booking system focus.


**Updating the Design**

I'm now zeroing in on UI consistency. My goal is to reflect the booking system across the application. I will update both the Navbar and Hero components. In the Navbar, I'm swapping "Consultoria AI" for "Marcação". The Hero component's button will change from "AI Consultant" to "Marcar". In `components/AIStyleConsultant.tsx`, I'll rename the component function to `BookingTerminal`, remove Gemini imports, and modify the state and logic to handle user input for booking.


**Refining the Navigation**

I'm now changing the navbar and hero components to reflect the booking system. In the navbar, I'm updating "Consultoria AI" to "Marcação". I'll also modify the scroll targets within the `Navbar.tsx` based on the old "Consultoria AI" ID. I'm keeping the ID as `consultoria` in the section to avoid breaking scrolling. In `Hero.tsx`, I'll update the button text to direct users to the booking feature.



**Implementing the Terminal**

I'm now focusing on the Terminal interaction for booking. Upon a user typing "marcar", I want to log a redirect message and then open the booking link in a new window. I'm keeping the same `AIStyleConsultant.tsx` file name for now. I'll be modifying the Navbar items and IDs. The Hero component's button text and target will be updated. The Terminal will now be the Booking section. The mock booking link will use `#`. I am targeting `Navbar.tsx` and the `Hero` file for the changes. I will be updating the component to handle the new action.

Para atender ao seu pedido, vou transformar a seção de "Consultoria AI" em uma área de "Marcação" (Booking), mantendo a estética do terminal.

**O que será alterado:**
1.  **`components/AIStyleConsultant.tsx`**:
    *   Remoção da lógica da IA (Gemini).
    *   Alteração dos textos para focar em agendamento.
    *   O terminal agora simula uma interface de comando (`CLI`) onde o usuário digita "marcar" para ser redirecionado para a agenda (simulada).
    *   ID da seção alterado para `marcacao`.
2.  **`components/Navbar.tsx`**:
    *   Item de menu "Consultoria AI" alterado para "Marcação".
3.  **`components/Hero.tsx`**:
    *   Botão "AI Consultant" alterado para "Agendar Agora" com redirecionamento para a nova seção.

entries.description: Atualização do link do menu de Consultoria para Marcação
Atualização do botão secundário para apontar para a seção de Marcação
